data.title: PC market sees strong Q3 growth amid Windows 10 transition, says Omdia
data.description:
Global shipments of desktops, notebooks, and workstations grew 6.8% year-over-year to 72M units in Q3 2025, according to the latest Omdia research. The uptick is driven by a wave of upgrade activity ahead of Microsoft’s Windows 10 end-of-support deadline on October 14.

Notebook shipments, including mobile workstations, rose 4% to 57.2 million units, while desktop shipments (including desktop workstations) surged 17% to 15.2 million units.

Top 5 PC Vendors – Q3 2025 

  • Lenovo (OTCPK:LNVGY) retained its lead in the global PC market, shipping approximately 19.4 million units, marking a 17% year-over-year increase.
  • HP (NYSE:HPQ) secured second place with 15 million units shipped, achieving 11% growth from the previous year.
  • Dell (NYSE:DELL) ranked third, showing modest annual growth of around 3%, supported by ongoing refresh demand.
  • Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) held fourth place, recording 4.3% growth and surpassing the 6-million unit mark for the fifth consecutive quarter.
  • Asus (OTCPK:ASUUY) rounded out the top five with 5.8 million units shipped, up 7% year-on-year.

Omdia Research Director Ishan Dutt noted that “business and consumer demand for PC upgrades continues to be healthy,” with many users still on older systems or running Windows 10.

Despite strong marketing efforts, a recent Omdia poll of 453 channel partners revealed only 39% have upgraded nearly all customer PCs, while 18% plan to remain on Windows 10 post-deadline—likely opting for Microsoft's (MSFT) extended security updates.

As upgrade demand extends into Q4 and beyond, chipmakers and OEMs are gearing up with new products. Qualcomm (QCOM) launched the Snapdragon X2 Elite chipset, and Intel (INTC) announced its next-gen Panther Lake Core Ultra processors.

Omdia predicts that CES 2026 will showcase the next wave of AI-enabled PCs.

data.title: Northwest Natural raises dividend by 0.5% to $0.4925 a share
data.description:
• Northwest Natural Holding (NYSE:NWN) declares $0.4925/share quarterly dividend, 0.5% increase from prior dividend of $0.4900.
  • Forward yield 4.51%
  • Payable Nov. 14; to shareholders of record Oct. 31; ex-div Oct. 31.
  • The company raised its quarterly dividend by 0.5% after paying a quarterly dividend of $0.49 in each of the previous 4 quarters.
  • See NWN Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Apogee beats top-line and bottom-line estimates; updates FY26 outlook
data.description:
• Apogee press release (NASDAQ:APOG): Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.98 beats by $0.14.
  • Revenue of $358.19M (+4.6% Y/Y) beats by $7.28M.
  • Adjusted EBITDA decreased to $44.4 million compared to $53.1 million, and adjusted EBITDA margin decreased to 12.4% compared to 15.5%.
  • Net cash provided by operating activities in the second quarter was $57.1 million, compared to $58.7 million in the prior-year period. 
  • Fiscal 2026 OutlookThe company now expects net sales in the range of $1.39 billion to $1.42 billion vs. consensus of $1.42B, and diluted EPS in the range of $2.79 to $3.19 and adjusted diluted EPS in the range of $3.60 to $3.90 vs. consensus of $3.96. This includes a projected unfavorable EPS impact from tariffs of $0.35 to $0.45. The company’s revised outlook assumes an effective tax rate of approximately 27%.
  • The company continues to assume capital expenditures between $35 million to $40 million.

data.title: Lockheed Martin raises dividend by 4.5% to $3.45 a share; approves $2B increase to share repurchase program
data.description:
• Lockheed Martin (NYSE:LMT) declares $3.45/share quarterly dividend, 4.5% increase from prior dividend of $3.30.
  • Forward yield 2.72%
  • Payable Dec. 30; to shareholders of record Dec. 1; ex-div Dec. 1.
  • The company's board officially approved adding $2 billion to its existing share repurchase program. Following this increase, Lockheed Martin now has roughly $9.1 billion authorized for future common stock repurchases.
  • The company raised its quarterly dividend by 4.5% after paying a quarterly dividend of $3.30 in each of the previous 4 quarters.
  • See LMT Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Senate passes bill requiring Nvidia, AMD to prioritize domestic customers over China
data.description:
The U.S. Senate on Thursday passed bipartisan legislation requiring advanced AI chipmakers such as Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) to prioritize American customers over Chinese buyers, a move that could complicate the industry’s push to expand global sales.

The Guaranteeing Access and Innovation for National Artificial Intelligence (GAIN AI) Act is a proposed bill that aims to give domestic companies priority access to advanced AI chips by requiring domestic orders to be fulfilled first before any exports.

The measure, approved as part of the annual defense policy bill, aims to strengthen U.S. competitiveness in emerging technologies and limit exports to China and other adversaries, according to co-sponsor Senator Jim Banks, an Indiana Republican.

“Today, the Senate acted to make sure American customers — including small businesses and startups — aren’t forced to wait in line behind China’s tech giants when purchasing the latest AI chips,” said Senator Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.), the bill’s Democratic co-sponsor.

Tech companies and trade groups have opposed the legislation, warning that it could stifle innovation and undercut U.S. chipmakers’ global competitiveness.

The bill’s future remains uncertain, as the House of Representatives passed its own version of the defense bill in September without the export-control provisions. Lawmakers from both chambers must now reconcile the differences before sending a final bill to President Donald Trump.

The Senate action comes months after the Trump administration reached a deal with Nvidia and AMD to ease chip export restrictions to China. Nvidia has argued the legislation targets a “nonexistent problem,” noting that the U.S. remains its largest market.

Still, advocates say the move is an important safeguard. The measure is a “major win for U.S. economic competitiveness and national security,” said Brad Carson, president of the nonprofit Americans for Responsible Innovation.

data.title: Friday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
9:45 AM Fed's Goolsbee Speech 
 
Chicago Federal Reserve Bank President Austan Goolsbee will give opening remarks and moderate a discussion before the 19th annual Community Bankers Symposium,"Community Banking: Charting the Course," hosted by the Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago.
 
10:00 AM Consumer Sentiment 
 
The University of Michigan's Consumer Survey Center questions households each month on their assessment of current conditions and expectations of future conditions.
 
The first reading for October is expected to show another decline to 54.0 from 55.1 in September and 58.2 in August as consumers fret over rising prices and weakening employment prospects.
 
1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count 
 
The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs. 
 
1:00 PM Fed's Musalem Speech 
 
St. Louis Federal Reserve Bank President Alberto Musalem will speak on the U.S. economy and monetary policy in fireside chat before the Springfield Area Chamber of Commerce Public Policy Speaker's Series.
 
2:00 PM Treasury Statement 
 
The U.S. Treasury releases a monthly account of the surplus or deficit of the federal government. Changes in the budget balance reflect federal policy on spending and taxation. The government's fiscal year begins in October.

data.title: Applied Digital targets $1B NOI run rate within 5 years as hyperscaler demand accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Applied Digital Corporation (APLD) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Wesley Cummins began by highlighting the company’s expanding role in AI and high-performance computing infrastructure, stating, "This quarter, we expanded our long-term lease agreements with CoreWeave, a publicly traded AI hyperscaler. Previously, we had 250 megawatts under contract at our Ellendale, North Dakota campus, Polaris Forge 1. That agreement represents approximately $7 billion in contracted revenue over 15 years. CoreWeave has since exercised its option and our leases now cover the full 400 megawatts of capacity currently under construction at Polaris Forge 1, increasing the total contract value to approximately $11 billion."

  • Cummins announced that Applied Digital broke ground on Polaris Forge 2, a new campus near Harwood, North Dakota, with initial plans for 300 megawatts and the potential to scale to 1 gigawatt. The initial development cost is projected at $3 billion, and the company is in discussions with multiple hyperscalers to support expansion.

  • Cummins emphasized speed, reliability, and readiness as critical differentiators, referencing industry-wide investment and the company’s early move into next-generation data center design.

  • Cummins noted, "We have now scaled to develop multiple campuses in parallel. This has resulted in us now having 700 megawatts currently under construction."

  • CFO Mohammad Saidal Mohmand reported, "We secured an initial $112.5 million draw from a $5 billion preferred equity facility with Macquarie Asset Management to advance construction of Polaris Forge 1. This structure is designed to fully finance the build-out and materially reduce future equity requirements across our platform."

Outlook

  • Management indicated that the company expects continued revenue ramp as the CoreWeave fit-out progresses, with full lease income recognition for the first 100-megawatt building anticipated toward the end of the calendar year.

  • Cummins stated, "We believe that a hyperscaler lease for Polaris Forge 2 would be a significant milestone for Applied Digital and the State of North Dakota. We think the 2 anchor customers under multi-billion long-term contracts would be a meaningful step toward reaching our low strengthening our position in the market and also establishing the region as a major hub for hyperscale infrastructure."

  • The company reasserted its goal of reaching a $1 billion NOI run rate within 5 years, supported by accelerating demand and long-term contracts.

Financial Results

  • CFO Mohmand stated, "Revenues for the first fiscal quarter of fiscal '26 were $64.2 million, up 84% from $34.8 million in the fiscal first quarter of 2025. The increase was primarily due to the $26.3 million of revenue generated from the tenant fit-out services associated with our HPC Hosting Business."

  • Cost of revenues was $55.6 million, with approximately $25 million linked to the tenant fit-out services. SG&A was $29.2 million, driven by a $16.6 million increase in stock-based compensation and $3.9 million in personnel expenses, partly offset by decreased professional service costs.

  • The company reported a net loss of $27.8 million or $0.11 per share, with an adjusted net loss of $7.6 million or $0.03 per share, and adjusted EBITDA of $0.5 million.

  • The balance sheet showed $114.1 million in cash, cash equivalents, and restricted cash, with $687.3 million in debt, not including $362.5 million in post-quarter financing proceeds.

Q&A

  • Nick Giles, B. Riley Securities: Asked about project financing structure and timing. CFO Mohmand responded that both buildings at Polaris Forge 1 are included in the financing process and described it as one of the largest CoreWeave tenant-backed financings in the market, aiming for terms similar or more optimal than competitor offerings.

  • Giles: Inquired about Polaris Forge 2’s power infrastructure. Cummins said 280 megawatts of initial utility power is in place and timelines for online dates remain as previously communicated.

  • Robert Brown, Lake Street Capital: Asked about new hyperscaler negotiations and expansion timelines. Cummins explained that negotiations are now a constant, with multiple campuses in progress and a 4-gigawatt active pipeline.

  • Brown: Questioned limiting factors for gigawatt-scale at campuses. Cummins responded that ramping depends on transmission infrastructure and grid generation, with careful matching to construction pace.

  • Mike Grondahl, Northland Capital: Asked about the strategic impact of $5 billion Macquarie financing. Cummins explained the facility enables scaling, minimizes dilution, and supports $20-$25 billion in total capital needs.

  • Grondahl: Inquired about project financing terms. Mohmand cited LTCs around 70% and pricing between 400-450 basis points over SOFR, with a bifurcated structure for mortgage and mezzanine portions.

  • Darren Aftahi, ROTH Capital: Sought details on active pipeline definition and human capital scaling. Cummins described the active pipeline as projects likely to move to construction within 6-12 months and highlighted investments in supply chain and labor pool to enable parallel campus development.

  • Logan Lillehaug, Craig-Hallum: Asked about lease economics at Harwood and expansion capability. Cummins confirmed similar economic spreads to Ellendale, with lower capital costs for investment-grade tenants.

  • Michael Donovan, Compass Point: Questioned supply chain conditions. Cummins said Applied Digital secured manufacturing capacity early, mitigating inflation and lead time pressures seen elsewhere in the industry.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were focused on financing progress, campus expansion, contract economics, and supply chain, with a neutral to slightly positive tone as they probed for details on execution and scalability.

  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing the company’s preparedness, strategic partnerships, and rapid scaling ability. Cummins asserted, "We remain confident in our strategy, our partnerships and our ability to lead this next chapter of digital infrastructure."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts exhibited increased confidence, with management more direct about long-term targets and expansion plans, while analysts pressed for specifics on execution and capital strategy.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from directional to more explicit targets, with management now openly reiterating the $1 billion NOI run rate goal within 5 years.

  • Strategic focus has advanced from initial contract wins to scaling multiple campuses and leveraging major financing arrangements.

  • Analysts’ questions evolved from project timelines and customer onboarding to deeper inquiries on financing structures and resource scalability.

  • Key metric changes include a substantial revenue increase driven by tenant fit-out services and a notable rise in SG&A due to stock-based compensation.

  • Management’s tone became more assertive around growth prospects, while analysts maintained a constructive but increasingly detailed approach.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management pointed to power availability shifting from a primary to a secondary concern as construction and scaling become the primary focus.

  • Supply chain risks were addressed through early procurement and manufacturing partnerships, with Cummins noting, "We did this some time ago where we've landed with these key partners."

  • Analysts raised questions about human capital, supply chain lead times, and the status of legislative incentives in South Dakota, to which management responded with mitigation strategies and ongoing negotiations.

Final Takeaway

Applied Digital underscored its transformation into a key enabler of AI and high-performance computing infrastructure, with long-term hyperscaler contracts and major financing secured to fuel rapid expansion. The company reaffirmed its $1 billion NOI run rate target within five years, highlighted robust demand, and outlined plans to scale multiple campuses in parallel, supported by strategic partnerships and supply chain investments. Management’s tone reflected increased confidence in execution capabilities and market positioning as the company moves to capture a greater share of the accelerating AI infrastructure build-out.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Park Aerospace outlines $40M–$45M facility expansion as defense demand accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Park Aerospace Corp. (PKE) Q2 2026

Management View

  • Brian Shore, Chairman & CEO, emphasized that Q2 sales reached $16.381 million with gross profit of $5.116 million and a gross margin of 31.2%. Shore noted, "we're happy about gross margins over 30% or maybe I should say we're unhappy when they're not over 30%." He highlighted coming in above the top of their previously stated sales and EBITDA estimates, while reiterating these are internal estimates rather than formal guidance.
  • Shore detailed the ongoing business partner agreement with ArianeGroup, reporting $1.65 million in C2B fabric sales and $415,000 in ablative materials manufactured with C2B fabric. He explained that defense industry customers are stockpiling C2B fabric, suppressing margins due to a higher proportion of lower-margin fabric sales.
  • Mark A. Esquivel, President & COO, reported progress on C2B fabric requalification, stating, "we do have approval. We don't have full approval. We have approval at about 90% of the specification... we're back in business, we're back running. And we're back to, I would say, normal typical rates that we were running prior to this."
  • Shore confirmed continued significant expenses related to operating the new manufacturing facility and highlighted a substantial increase in missed shipments to $510,000, attributing this to customer certification and testing delays.
  • The company is advancing a major expansion of manufacturing capacity, estimating a capital budget of $40 million to $45 million, up from previous estimates, to support both GE Aerospace and missile defense program growth.
  • Esquivel assured that tariff-related costs were minimal and largely passed through to customers: "I don't expect to see much... it's very minimal impact to our business."

Outlook

  • Shore provided updated estimates for GE Aerospace jet engine program sales for the year at $27.5 million to $29 million, revising down from a prior estimate of $28 million to $32 million, explaining, "that's now Park forecast based upon what -- based upon the backlog for Q3 and Q4."
  • For Q3 2026, the company estimates sales of $16.5 million to $17.5 million and adjusted EBITDA of $3.7 million to $4.1 million.
  • Shore signaled that for fiscal 2026, revenue is expected to be "over $70 million."
  • No formal long-term guidance was provided, but management indicated more perspective may be shared next quarter.

Financial Results

  • Q2 sales were $16.381 million, above the internal estimate range of $15 million to $16 million provided last quarter.
  • Gross margin was 31.2%, with adjusted EBITDA of $3.401 million, at the top end of prior estimates.
  • The company reported $61.6 million in cash and marketable securities at quarter end, following a $4.9 million tax payment.
  • No shares were repurchased during Q2 or Q3 to date.
  • The ongoing C2B fabric sales mix continued to weigh on gross margins, though high-margin ablative material sales provided some offset.

Q&A

  • Nick Ripostella, NR Management, asked about sales staffing, long-term forecasts, and research coverage. Shore responded that their approach favors technical and engineering hires over salespeople, stating, "they're more interested in what you can do, how you can help us. And that's going to be more of an engineering discussion."
  • Ripostella inquired about longer-term forecasts. Shore indicated, "I think what we'll try to do in Q3 is provide some information. I'm a little bit -- like a little reluctant because I think the number is going to be shocking to our investors."
  • A private investor asked about the margin mix of C2B fabric and the timing of higher-revenue converted material sales. Shore explained that OEMs are currently stockpiling, but "eventually, the plan is not to just have that stuff sitting in our factory... it's for us to produce the material that's used to make the rocket nozzle materials for the -- rocket nozzle structures for the Patriot missile system," and expected increased activity as requalification issues have largely been resolved.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed optimism about growth prospects and recognition for the company's performance, with Ripostella noting, "you truly are a growth company."
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, repeatedly referencing "once-in-a-lifetime" opportunities and a strong positioning in key aerospace and defense programs.
  • During Q&A, management was open but cautious, particularly around providing long-term forecasts, with Shore noting reluctance due to potentially surprising figures.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone has shifted to even greater confidence in new opportunities and program ramp-ups, while maintaining a conservative stance on public forward guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The revised GE Aerospace jet engine program sales forecast ($27.5 million to $29 million) is lower than last quarter’s $28 million to $32 million projection, reflecting a move from customer-provided to company-validated estimates.
  • The capital budget for facility expansion increased from $35–$40 million to $40–$45 million, driven by rising requirements.
  • Management tone remains confident, with increasing emphasis on defense sector opportunities and urgent facility expansion.
  • Analyst focus has shifted from clarifying LTA terms and buyback policy to questions about scaling, staffing, and long-term revenue potential.

Risks and Concerns

  • Customer certification and testing delays led to $510,000 in missed shipments, a notable increase from prior quarters.
  • The ongoing mix of lower-margin C2B fabric sales versus high-margin converted products continues to affect gross margin.
  • Management acknowledged that significant ongoing expenses from operating the new facility are not expected to abate soon.
  • Tariff risk is monitored but currently not impactful due to effective pass-through strategies.

Final Takeaway

Park Aerospace management emphasized the company’s robust position in aerospace and defense markets, highlighting accelerated demand for missile programs and a major facility expansion with a capital budget of $40 million to $45 million. Revised sales estimates reflect a more prudent approach, while the leadership team signals that substantial growth opportunities are within reach, supported by strong balance sheet discipline and a strategy focused on technical excellence and responsiveness to customer needs.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Levi Strauss signals 6% organic net revenue growth for 2025 while raising EPS outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Levi Strauss & Co. (LEVI) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Michelle Gass, CEO, announced "we delivered another very strong quarter with upside across the P&L giving us the confidence to raise our full year revenue and EPS guidance." Gass emphasized the company's "fourth consecutive quarter of high single-digit organic revenue growth" with strength across direct-to-consumer (DTC) and wholesale, international and domestic markets, and both men's and women's segments. She attributed the performance to "continued strong sales and profitability in our direct-to-consumer channel, up 9% and fueled by strong comp growth as well as solid performance in global wholesale."

  • Gass highlighted international expansion, stating "our International business was up 9%, led by an acceleration in Asia," and noted robust growth in women's and tops categories. She detailed recent marketing efforts, including the REIIMAGINE campaign with Beyonce and a collaboration with Nike, which "delivered as intended, fueling momentum across the business, specifically driving growth in our Levi's Women's business up 12% year-to-date."

  • Harmit Singh, Executive VP & Chief Financial & Growth Officer, stated, "In quarter 3, we delivered strong financial results exceeding expectations across sales, gross margin, EBIT margin and EPS." Singh added, "Given the outperformance in quarter 3 and continued strong trends, we are also raising our revenue and EPS outlook for the year, despite incorporating higher tariffs than assumed in our previous guidance."

Outlook

  • Singh shared, "For the full year, we are taking our revenues up by approximately 1 percentage point and EPS by $0.02. We now expect reported net revenue growth of approximately 3% for the year, and we have increased our expectations for organic net revenues to approximately 6% up from prior year."

  • He further projected, "We now expect gross margin to expand 100 basis points for the full year, up from the 80 basis points stated in our prior guidance, including the incremental drag from tariffs."

  • For Q4, Singh stated, "We expect organic net revenue growth to be up approximately 1%." Adjusted diluted EPS guidance for the full year was raised to a range of "$1.27 to $1.32."

Financial Results

  • Singh stated, "Net revenue grew 7%, reflecting the power of our diversified business model. International markets drove approximately 75% of our growth and the U.S. contributed 25%."

  • Gross margin for Q3 reached "a quarter 3 record gross margin of 61.7% of net revenues, expanding 110 basis points versus the prior year, more than offsetting 80 basis points of tariff headwinds."

  • "We delivered an adjusted EBIT margin of 11.8% and generated $0.34 of adjusted diluted EPS, both ahead of our expectations," according to Singh. The company "returned $151 million to shareholders, a 118% increase versus last year," and declared a Q4 dividend of "$0.14 per share, which is up 8% to prior year."

Q&A

  • Laurent Vasilescu, BNP Paribas Exane, asked about European momentum and gross margin impacts. Singh replied, "Europe was up 3% for the quarter... DTC was up 4%, wholesale was up 2%." He added, "On Q4, we expect the momentum of our business to continue... Our Q4 guidance overall is impacted by 3 things: the year we're lapping, which includes a 53rd week, incremental headwind on tariff, and a conservative approach given the complex macros."

  • Matthew Boss, JPMorgan, inquired about category momentum and demand trends. Singh responded, "No, it's just being -- the prudent guidance is just being conservatism on the macros. We are not seeing any underlying change in trends." Gass stated, "The denim category is accelerating both here in the U.S. and globally. As the definitive market leader, we are very well positioned to take advantage of that."

  • Irwin Boruchow, Wells Fargo, asked about SG&A and distribution costs. Singh explained, "Distribution costs, about 7%, and they've increased from a year ago. I would say about half of that is a reclass in distribution expenses from selling to distribution for e-commerce."

  • Paul Kearney, Barclays, focused on wholesale growth drivers. Gass noted, "The growth is largely being driven with existing accounts as their consumers are responding to our fashion fits; women's, women's especially is outperforming; and lifestyle."

  • Oliver Chen, TD Cowen, asked about Americas growth and SKU rationalization. Gass replied, "We've decreased our SKUs by about 15% compared to last year... 40% of our SKUs are globally common."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a generally positive tone, focusing on sustainability of growth, margin drivers, and inventory management. There was a note of prudence regarding macro headwinds and tariff impacts.

  • Management’s tone was confident during prepared remarks, with repeated references to "momentum," "resilience," and "strong performance." During Q&A, Singh and Gass maintained composure, often emphasizing prudent planning and resilience, e.g., "No, it's just being -- the prudent guidance is just being conservatism on the macros."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, the overall tone remains upbeat, with slightly increased confidence following strong results and raised guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for full-year organic net revenue growth was raised from a range of 4.5%-5.5% to approximately 6%.

  • Margin expansion guidance increased from 80 basis points to 100 basis points.

  • Adjusted diluted EPS outlook moved from $1.25-$1.30 to $1.27-$1.32.

  • Both quarters showed strong DTC and wholesale momentum, but Q3 guidance and commentary reflected even greater confidence in sustaining growth and profitability despite higher tariff headwinds.

  • Analysts in both quarters pressed on sustainability, margin levers, and inventory, but current quarter responses were more detailed about mitigation strategies and SKU rationalization.

Risks and Concerns

  • Singh acknowledged higher tariffs, stating, "Our updated guidance reflects the latest tariff rates, which include 30% for China and an increase to approximately 20% for the rest of the world." He estimated a "full year gross impact of tariffs before mitigation to be approximately a 70 basis point headwind to gross margin."

  • Inventory was noted as up, with Singh explaining, "We ended the quarter with reported inventory dollars up 12%, driven by purposeful investments ahead of the holiday and higher product costs than a year ago due to tariffs."

  • Management outlined mitigation initiatives including "promotion optimization, targeted pricing actions, vendor negotiation and further supply chain diversification."

Final Takeaway

Levi Strauss & Co. delivered another strong quarter marked by broad-based revenue growth, record gross margins, and confident execution of its DTC-first strategy. Management raised full-year revenue and EPS guidance, citing robust international expansion and successful product innovation. Despite increased tariff headwinds and macro uncertainty, the company remains confident in its ability to drive profitable growth through disciplined execution, inventory management, and targeted mitigation strategies, positioning itself for continued momentum into 2026.
